content.text:
Please analyze this codebase and create a CLAUDE.md file, which will be given to future instances of Claude Code to operate in this repository.

What to add:
1. Commands that will be commonly used, such as how to build, lint, and run tests. Include the necessary commands to develop in this codebase, such as how to run a single test.
2. High-level code architecture and structure so that future instances can be productive more quickly. Focus on the "big picture" architecture that requires reading multiple files to understand.

Usage notes:
- If there's already a CLAUDE.md, suggest improvements to it.
- When you make the initial CLAUDE.md, do not repeat yourself and do not include obvious instructions like "Provide helpful error messages to users", "Write unit tests for all new utilities", "Never include sensitive information (API keys, tokens) in code or commits".
- Avoid listing every component or file structure that can be easily discovered.
- Don't include generic development practices.
- If there are Cursor rules (in .cursor/rules/ or .cursorrules) or Copilot rules (in .github/copilot-instructions.md), make sure to include the important parts.
- If there is a README.md, make sure to include the important parts.
- Do not make up information such as "Common Development Tasks", "Tips for Development", "Support and Documentation" unless this is expressly included in other files that you read.
- Be sure to prefix the file with the following text:

```
# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.
```

-# Workflow Quick Reference
-
-**Model:** Contractor (you direct, Claude orchestrates)
-
----
-
-## The Loop
-
-```
-Your instruction
-    ↓
-[PLAN] (if multi-file or unclear) → Show plan → Your approval
-    ↓
-[EXECUTE] Implement, verify, done
-    ↓
-[REPORT] Summary + what's ready
-    ↓
-Repeat
-```
-
----
-
-## I Ask You When
-
-- **Design forks:** "Option A (fast) vs. Option B (robust). Which?"
-- **Code ambiguity:** "Spec unclear on X. Assume Y?"
-- **Replication edge case:** "Just missed tolerance. Investigate?"
-- **Scope question:** "Also refactor Y while here, or focus on X?"
-
----
-
-## I Just Execute When
-
-- Code fix is obvious (bug, pattern application)
-- Verification (tolerance checks, tests, compilation)
-- Documentation (logs, commits)
-- Plotting (per established standards)
-- Deployment (after you approve, I ship automatically)
-
----
-
-## Quality Gates (No Exceptions)
-
-| Score | Action |
-|-------|--------|
-| >= 80 | Ready to commit |
-| < 80  | Fix blocking issues |
-
----
-
-## Non-Negotiables (Customize These)
-
-<!-- Replace with YOUR project's locked-in preferences -->
-
-- [YOUR PATH CONVENTION] (e.g., `here::here()` for R, relative paths for LaTeX)
-- [YOUR SEED CONVENTION] (e.g., `set.seed()` once at top for stochastic code)
-- [YOUR FIGURE STANDARDS] (e.g., white bg, 300 DPI, custom theme)
-- [YOUR COLOR PALETTE] (e.g., institutional colors)
-- [YOUR TOLERANCE THRESHOLDS] (e.g., 1e-6 for point estimates)
-
----
-
-## Preferences
-
-<!-- Fill in as you discover your working style -->
-
-**Visual:** [How you want figures/plots handled]
...
</persisted-output>

0→# My Claude Code Setup 1→ 2→> **Work in progress.** This is not meant to be a polished guide for everyone. It's mostly a summary of how I've been using Claude Code for academic work — creating lecture slides, writing R scripts, managing Beamer-to-Quarto workflows, and so on. I keep learning new things, and as I do, I keep updating these files. This is just a way for me to share what I've figured out with friends and colleagues. 3→ 4→**Live site:** [psantanna.com/claude-code-my-workflow](https://psantanna.com/claude-code-my-workflow/) 5→**Last Updated:** 2026-02-15 6→ 7→A ready-to-fork starter kit for academics using [Claude Code](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/overview) with **LaTeX/Beamer + R + Quarto**. You describe what you want; Claude plans the approach, runs specialized agents, fixes issues, verifies quality, and presents results — like a contractor who handles the entire job. Extracted from a production PhD course (6 lectures, 800+ slides). 8→ 9→--- 10→ 11→## Quick Start (5 minutes) 12→ 13→### 1. Fork & Clone 14→ 15→```bash 16→# Fork this repo on GitHub (click "Fork" on the repo page), then: 17→git clone https://github.com/YOUR_USERNAME/claude-code-my-workflow.git my-project 18→cd my-project 19→``` 20→ 21→Replace `YOUR_USERNAME` with your GitHub username. 22→ 23→### 2. Start Claude Code and Paste This Prompt 24→ 25→```bash 26→claude 27→``` 28→ 29→**Using VS Code?** Open the Claude Code panel instead. Everything works the same — see the [full guide](https://psantanna.com/claude-code-my-workflow/workflow-guide.html#sec-setup) for details. 30→ 31→Then paste the following, filling in your project details: 32→ 33→> I am starting to work on **[PROJECT NAME]** in this repo. **[Describe your project in 2–3 sentences — what you're building, who it's for, what tools you use.]** 34→> 35→> I want our collaboration to be structured, precise, and rigorous. When creating visuals, everything must be polished and publication-ready. 36→> 37→> I've set up the Claude Code academic workflow (forked from `pedrohcgs/claude-code-my-workflow`). The configuration files are already in this repo. Please read them, understand the workflow, and then **update all configuration files to fit my project** — fill in placeholders in `CLAUDE.md`, adjust rules if needed, and propose any customizations specific to my use case. 38→> 39→> After that, use the plan-first workflow for all non-trivial tasks. Once I approve a plan, switch to contractor mode — coordinate everything autonomously and only come back to me when there's ambiguity or a decision to make. 40→> 41→> Enter plan mode and start by adapting the workflow configuration for this project. 42→ 43→**What this does:** Claude reads all the configuration files, fills in your project name, institution, and preferences, then enters contractor mode — planning, implementing, reviewing, and verifying autonomously. You approve the plan and Claude handles the rest. 44→ 45→**Prefer to configure manually?** See the [full guide](https://psantanna.com/claude-code-my-workflow/workflow-guide.html#sec-setup) for step-by-step manual setup instructions. 46→ 47→--- 48→ 49→## How It Works 50→ 51→### Contractor Mode 52→ 53→You describe a task. For complex or ambiguous requests, Claude first creates a requirements specification with MUST/SHOULD/MAY priorities and clarity status (CLEAR/ASSUMED/BLOCKED). You approve the spec, then Claude plans the approach, implements it, runs specialized review agents, fixes issues, re-verifies, and scores against quality gates — all autonomously. You see a summary when the work meets quality standards. Say "just do it" and it auto-commits too. 54→ 55→### Specialized Agents 56→ 57→Instead of one general-purpose reviewer, 10 focused agents each check one dimension: 58→ 59→- **proofreader** — grammar/typos 60→- **slide-auditor** — visual layout 61→- **pedagogy-reviewer** — teaching quality 62→- **r-reviewer** — R code quality 63→- **domain-reviewer** — field-specific correctness (template — customize for your field) 64→ 65→Each is better at its narrow task than a generalist would be. The `/slide-excellence` skill runs them all in parallel. 66→ 67→### Adversarial QA 68→ 69→Two agents work in opposition: the **critic** reads both Beamer and Quarto and produces harsh findings. The **fixer** implements exactly what the critic found. They loop until the critic says "APPROVED" (or 5 rounds max). This catches errors that single-pass review misses. 70→ 71→### Quality Gates 72→ 73→Every file gets a score (0–100). Scores below threshold block the action: 74→- **80** — commit threshold 75→- **90** — PR threshold 76→- **95** — excellence (aspirational) 77→ 78→### Context Survival 79→ 80→Plans, specifications, and session logs survive auto-compression and session boundaries. The PreCompact hook saves a context snapshot before Claude's auto-compression triggers, ensuring critical decisions are never lost. MEMORY.md accumulates learning across sessions, so patterns discovered in one session inform future work. 81→ 82→--- 83→ 84→## The Guide 85→ 86→For a comprehensive walkthrough, read the **[full guide](https://psantanna.com/claude-code-my-workflow/workflow-guide.html)** (or see the [source](guide/workflow-guide.qmd)). 87→ 88→It covers: 89→1. **Why This Workflow Exists** — the problem and the vision 90→2. **Getting Started** — fork, paste one prompt, and Claude sets up the rest 91→3. **The System in Action** — specialized agents, adversarial QA, quality scoring 92→4. **The Building Blocks** — CLAUDE.md, rules, skills, agents, hooks, memory 93→5. **Workflow Patterns** — lecture creation, translation, replication, multi-agent review, research exploration 94→6. **Customizing for Your Domain** — creating your own reviewers and knowledge bases 95→ 96→--- 97→ 98→## What's Included 99→ 100→
101→10 agents, 21 skills, 17 rules, 7 hooks (click to expand) 102→ 103→### Agents (`.claude/agents/`) 104→ 105→| Agent | What It Does | 106→|-------|-------------| 107→| `proofreader` | Grammar, typos, overflow, consistency review | 108→| `slide-auditor` | Visual layout audit (overflow, font consistency, spacing) | 109→| `pedagogy-reviewer` | 13-pattern pedagogical review (narrative arc, notation density, pacing) | 110→| `r-reviewer` | R code quality, reproducibility, and domain correctness | 111→| `tikz-reviewer` | Merciless TikZ diagram visual critique | 112→| `beamer-translator` | Beamer-to-Quarto translation specialist | 113→| `quarto-critic` | Adversarial QA comparing Quarto against Beamer benchmark | 114→| `quarto-fixer` | Implements fixes from the critic agent | 115→| `verifier` | End-to-end task completion verification | 116→| `domain-reviewer` | **Template** for your field-specific substance reviewer | 117→ 118→### Skills (`.claude/skills/`) 119→ 120→| Skill | `/translate-to-quarto` | Full 11-phase Beamer-to-Quarto translation | 132→| `/validate-bib` | Cross-reference citations against bibliography | 133→| `/devils-advocate` | Challenge design decisions before committing | 134→| `/create-lecture` | Full lecture creation workflow | 135→| `/commit` | Stage, commit, create PR, and merge to main | 136→| `/lit-review` | Literature search, synthesis, and gap identification | 137→| `/research-ideation` | Generate research questions and empirical strategies | 138→| `/interview-me` | Interactive interview to formalize a research idea | 139→| `/review-paper` | Manuscript review: structure, econometrics, referee objections | 140→| `/data-analysis` | End-to-end R analysis with publication-ready output | 141→| `/learn` | Extract non-obvious discoveries into persistent skills | 142→| `/context-status` | Show session health and context usage | 143→ 144→### Research Workflow 145→ 146→| Feature | What It Does | 147→|---------|-------------| Repository structure (absolute paths under `/Users/my/.claude/worktrees/agent-aea0751b/.Trash/my-project 19.06.43`) is intentionally split between governance/configuration and a working academic workflow:

- `README.md` (`/Users/my/.claude/.../README.md`) describes the overall workflow, agent/skill catalog, prerequisites (Claude Code CLI, XeLaTeX, Quarto, R, pdf2svg, gh CLI), and links to the full HTML guide (`docs/workflow-guide.html` and `guide/workflow-guide.qmd`), making it the canonical onboarding doc.
- `CLAUDE.md` (`/.../CLAUDE.md`) is the main project memory template: it outlines tolerances (plan-first, quality gates, Beamer = source of truth), folder map (`Slides/`, `Quarto/`, `scripts/`, etc.), standard build commands (XeLaTeX 3-pass with bibtex, `scripts/sync_to_docs.sh` for Quarto deploy, `python scripts/quality_score.py` for scoring), and quick-reference skills.
- Governance artifacts live under `.claude/`: 
  - `rules/` (e.g., `plan-first-workflow.md`, `orchestrator-protocol.md`, `session-logging.md`, `knowledge-base-template.md`, etc.) define when to plan, orchestrate, log, and constrain edits to source materials.
  - `agents/` and `skills/` folders enumerate specialized agents (proofreader, slide-auditor, domain reviewer, etc.) and CLI-style skills (`/compile-latex`, `/deploy`, `/proofread`, `/slide-excellence`, `/translate-to-quarto`, `/data-analysis`, etc.).
  - `hooks/` and supporting docs like `WORKFLOW_QUICK_REF.md` coordinate pre-commit automation and quick references.
- Supporting templates (under `/Users/my/.claude/.../templates/`) such as `requirements-spec.md`, `quality-report.md`, `session-log.md`, and `constitutional-governance.md` standardize plans, specs, and logging expected in `quality_reports/`.
- `scripts/` includes automation utilities (`scripts/sync_to_docs.sh`, `scripts/quality_score.py`, and the `scripts/R/` directory for reproducible analytics).
- `Slides/`, `Quarto/`, `Figures/`, and `master_supporting_docs/` house the LaTeX source decks (Beamer), Quarto equivalents, figure assets, and supporting papers; Quarto uses a theme (`Quarto/emory-clean.scss`) referenced in the guide.

1→# My Claude Code Setup 2→ 3→> **Work in progress.** This is not meant to be a polished guide for everyone. It's mostly a summary of how I've been using Claude Code for academic work — creating lecture slides, writing R scripts, managing Beamer-to-Quarto workflows, and so on. I keep learning new things, and as I do, I keep updating these files. This is just a way for me to share what I've figured out with friends and colleagues. 4→ 5→**Live site:** [psantanna.com/claude-code-my-workflow](https://psantanna.com/claude-code-my-workflow/) 6→**Last Updated:** 2026-02-15 7→ 8→A ready-to-fork starter kit for academics using [Claude Code](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/overview) with **LaTeX/Beamer + R + Quarto**. You describe what you want; Claude plans the approach, runs specialized agents, fixes issues, verifies quality, and presents results — like a contractor who handles the entire job. Extracted from a production PhD course (6 lectures, 800+ slides). 9→ 10→--- 11→ 12→## Quick Start (5 minutes) 13→ 14→### 1. Fork & Clone 15→ 16→```bash 17→# Fork this repo on GitHub (click "Fork" on the repo page), then: 18→git clone https://github.com/YOUR_USERNAME/claude-code-my-workflow.git my-project 19→cd my-project 20→``` 21→ 22→Replace `YOUR_USERNAME` with your GitHub username. 23→ 24→### 2. Start Claude Code and Paste This Prompt 25→ 26→```bash 27→claude 28→``` 29→ 30→**Using VS Code?** Open the Claude Code panel instead. Everything works the same — see the [full guide](https://psantanna.com/claude-code-my-workflow/workflow-guide.html#sec-setup) for details. 31→ 32→Then paste the following, filling in your project details: 33→ 34→> I am starting to work on **[PROJECT NAME]** in this repo. **[Describe your project in 2–3 sentences — what you're building, who it's for, what tools you use.]** 35→> 36→> I want our collaboration to be structured, precise, and rigorous. When creating visuals, everything must be polished and publication-ready. 37→> 38→> I've set up the Claude Code academic workflow (forked from `pedrohcgs/claude-code-my-workflow`). The configuration files are already in this repo. Please read them, understand the workflow, and then **update all configuration files to fit my project** — fill in placeholders in `CLAUDE.md`, adjust rules if needed, and propose any customizations specific to my use case. 39→> 40→> After that, use the plan-first workflow for all non-trivial tasks. Once I approve a plan, switch to contractor mode — coordinate everything autonomously and only come back to me when there's ambiguity or a decision to make. 41→> 42→> Enter plan mode and start by adapting the workflow configuration for this project. 43→ 44→**What this does:** Claude reads all the configuration files, fills in your project name, institution, and preferences, then enters contractor mode — planning, implementing, reviewing, and verifying autonomously. You approve the plan and Claude handles the rest. 45→ 46→**Prefer to configure manually?** See the [full guide](https://psantanna.com/claude-code-my-workflow/workflow-guide.html#sec-setup) for step-by-step manual setup instructions. 47→ 48→--- 49→ 50→## How It Works 51→ 52→### Contractor Mode 53→ 54→You describe a task. For complex or ambiguous requests, Claude first creates a requirements specification with MUST/SHOULD/MAY priorities and clarity status (CLEAR/ASSUMED/BLOCKED). You approve the spec, then Claude plans the approach, implements it, runs specialized review agents, fixes issues, re-verifies, and scores against quality gates — all autonomously. You see a summary when the work meets quality standards. Say "just do it" and it auto-commits too. 55→ 56→### Specialized Agents 57→ 58→Instead of one general-purpose reviewer, 10 focused agents each check one dimension: 59→ 60→- **proofreader** — grammar/typos 61→- **slide-auditor** — visual layout 62→- **pedagogy-reviewer** — teaching quality 63→- **r-reviewer** — R code quality 64→- **domain-reviewer** — field-specific correctness (template — customize for your field) 65→ 66→Each is better at its narrow task than a generalist would be. The `/slide-excellence` skill runs them all in parallel. 67→ 68→### Adversarial QA 69→ 70→Two agents work in opposition: the **critic** reads both Beamer and Quarto and produces harsh findings. The **fixer** implements exactly what the critic found. They loop until the critic says "APPROVED" (or 5 rounds max). This catches errors that single-pass review misses. 71→ 72→### Quality Gates 73→ 74→Every file gets a score (0–100). # My Claude Code Setup > **Work in progress.** This is not meant to be a polished guide for everyone. It's mostly a summary of how I've been using Claude Code for academic work — creating lecture slides, writing R scripts, managing Beamer-to-Quarto workflows, and so on. I keep learning new things, and as I do, I keep updating these files. This is just a way for me to share what I've figured out with friends and colleagues. **Live site:** [psantanna.com/claude-code-my-workflow](https://psantanna.com/claude-code-my-workflow/) **Last Updated:** 2026-02-15 A ready-to-fork starter kit for academics using [Claude Code](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/overview) with **LaTeX/Beamer + R + Quarto**. You describe what you want; Claude plans the approach, runs specialized agents, fixes issues, verifies quality, and presents results — like a contractor who handles the entire job. Extracted from a production PhD course (6 lectures, 800+ slides). --- ## Quick Start (5 minutes) ### 1. Fork & Clone ```bash # Fork this repo on GitHub (click "Fork" on the repo page), then: git clone https://github.com/YOUR_USERNAME/claude-code-my-workflow.git my-project cd my-project ``` Replace `YOUR_USERNAME` with your GitHub username. ### 2. Start Claude Code and Paste This Prompt ```bash claude ``` **Using VS Code?** Open the Claude Code panel instead. Everything works the same — see the [full guide](https://psantanna.com/claude-code-my-workflow/workflow-guide.html#sec-setup) for details. Then paste the following, filling in your project details: > I am starting to work on **[PROJECT NAME]** in this repo. **[Describe your project in 2–3 sentences — what you're building, who it's for, what tools you use.]** > > I want our collaboration to be structured, precise, and rigorous. When creating visuals, everything must be polished and publication-ready. > > I've set up the Claude Code academic workflow (forked from `pedrohcgs/claude-code-my-workflow`). The configuration files are already in this repo. Please read them, understand the workflow, and then **update all configuration files to fit my project** — fill in placeholders in `CLAUDE.md`, adjust rules if needed, and propose any customizations specific to my use case. > > After that, use the plan-first workflow for all non-trivial tasks. Once I approve a plan, switch to contractor mode — coordinate everything autonomously and only come back to me when there's ambiguity or a decision to make. > > Enter plan mode and start by adapting the workflow configuration for this project. **What this does:** Claude reads all the configuration files, fills in your project name, institution, and preferences, then enters contractor mode — planning, implementing, reviewing, and verifying autonomously. You approve the plan and Claude handles the rest. **Prefer to configure manually?** See the [full guide](https://psantanna.com/claude-code-my-workflow/workflow-guide.html#sec-setup) for step-by-step manual setup instructions. --- ## How It Works ### Contractor Mode You describe a task. For complex or ambiguous requests, Claude first creates a requirements specification with MUST/SHOULD/MAY priorities and clarity status (CLEAR/ASSUMED/BLOCKED). You approve the spec, then Claude plans the approach, implements it, runs specialized review agents, fixes issues, re-verifies, and scores against quality gates — all autonomously. You see a summary when the work meets quality standards. Say "just do it" and it auto-commits too. ### Specialized Agents Instead of one general-purpose reviewer, 10 focused agents each check one dimension: - **proofreader** — grammar/typos - **slide-auditor** — visual layout - **pedagogy-reviewer** — teaching quality - **r-reviewer** — R code quality - **domain-reviewer** — field-specific correctness (template — customize for your field) Each is better at its narrow task than a generalist would be. The `/slide-excellence` skill runs them all in parallel. ### Adversarial QA Two agents work in opposition: the **critic** reads both Beamer and Quarto and produces harsh findings. The **fixer** implements exactly what the critic found. They loop until the critic says "APPROVED" (or 5 rounds max). This catches errors that single-pass review misses. ### Quality Gates Every file gets a score (0–100). Scores below threshold block the action: - **80** — commit threshold - **90** — PR threshold - **95** — excellence (aspirational) ### Context Survival Plans, specifications, and session logs survive auto-compression and session boundaries. The PreCompact hook saves a context snapshot before Claude's auto-compression triggers, ensuring critical decisions are never lost. MEMORY.md accumulates learning across sessions, so patterns discovered in one session inform future work. --- ## The Guide For a comprehensive walkthrough, read the **[full guide](https://psantanna.com/claude-code-my-workflow/workflow-guide.html)** (or see the [source](guide/workflow-guide.qmd)). It covers: 1. **Why This Workflow Exists** — the problem and the vision 2. **Getting Started** — fork, paste one prompt, and Claude sets up the rest 3. **The System in Action** — specialized agents, adversarial QA, quality scoring 4. **The Building Blocks** — CLAUDE.md, rules, skills, agents, hooks, memory 5. **Workflow Patterns** — lecture creation, translation, replication, multi-agent review, research exploration 6. **Customizing for Your Domain** — creating your own reviewers and knowledge bases --- ## What's Included
10 agents, 21 skills, 17 rules, 7 hooks (click to expand) ### Agents (`.claude/agents/`) | Agent | What It Does | |-------|-------------| | `proofreader` | Grammar, typos, overflow, consistency review | | `slide-auditor` | Visual layout audit (overflow, font consistency, spacing) | | `pedagogy-reviewer` | 13-pattern pedagogical review (narrative arc, notation density, pacing) | | `r-reviewer` | R code quality, reproducibility, and domain correctness | | `tikz-reviewer` | Merciless TikZ diagram visual critique | | `beamer-translator` | Beamer-to-Quarto translation specialist | | `quarto-critic` | Adversarial QA comparing Quarto against Beamer benchmark | | `quarto-fixer` | Implements fixes from the critic agent | | `verifier` | End-to-end task completion verification | | `domain-reviewer` | **Template** for your field-specific substance reviewer | ### Skills (`.claude/skills/`) | Skill | What It Does | |-------|-------------| | `/compile-latex` | 3-pass XeLaTeX compilation with bibtex | | `/deploy` | Render Quarto + sync to GitHub Pages | | `/extract-tikz` | TikZ diagrams to PDF to SVG pipeline | | `/proofread` | Launch proofreader on a file | | `/visual-audit` | Launch slide-auditor on a file | | `/pedagogy-review` | Launch pedagogy-reviewer on a file | | `/review-r` | Launch R code reviewer | | `/qa-quarto` | Adversarial critic-fixer loop (max 5 rounds) | | `/slide-excellence` | Combined multi-agent review | | `/translate-to-quarto` | Full 11-phase Beamer-to-Quarto translation | | `/validate-bib` | Cross-reference citations against bibliography | | `/devils-advocate` | Challenge design decisions before committing | | `/create-lecture` | Full lecture creation workflow | | `/commit` | Stage, commit, create PR, and merge to main | | `/lit-review` | Literature search, synthesis, and gap identification | | `/research-ideation` | Generate research questions and empirical strategies | | `/interview-me` | Interactive interview to formalize a research idea | | `/review-paper` | Manuscript review: structure, econometrics, referee objections | | `/data-analysis` | End-to-end R analysis with publication-ready output | | `/learn` | Extract non-obvious discoveries into persistent skills | | `/context-status` | Show session health and context usage | ### Research Workflow | Feature | What It Does | |---------|-------------| | Exploration folder | Structured `explorations/` sandbox with graduate/archive lifecycle | | Fast-track workflow | 60/100 quality threshold for rapid prototyping | | Simplified orchestrator | implement → verify → score → done (no multi-round reviews) | | Enhanced session logging | Structured tables for changes, decisions, verification | | Merge-only reporting | Quality reports at merge time only | | Math line-length exception | Long lines acceptable for documented formulas | | Workflow quick reference | One-page cheat sheet at `.claude/WORKFLOW_QUICK_REF.md` | ### Rules (`.claude/rules/`) Rules use path-scoped loading: **always-on** rules load every session (~100 lines total); **path-scoped** rules load only when Claude works on matching files. Claude follows ~150 instructions reliably, so less is more. **Always-on** (no `paths:` frontmatter — load every session): | Rule | What It Enforces | |------|-----------------| | `plan-first-workflow` | Plan mode for non-trivial tasks + context preservation | | `orchestrator-protocol` | Contractor mode: implement → verify → review → fix → score | | `session-logging` | Three logging triggers: post-plan, incremental, end-of-session | **Path-scoped** (load only when working on matching files): | Rule | Triggers On | What It Enforces | |------|------------|-----------------| | `verification-protocol` | `.tex`, `.qmd`, `docs/` | Task completion checklist | | `single-source-of-truth` | `Figures/`, `.tex`, `.qmd` | No content duplication; Beamer is authoritative | | `quality-gates` | `.tex`, `.qmd`, `*.R` | 80/90/95 scoring + tolerance thresholds | | `r-code-conventions` | `*.R` | R coding standards + math line-length exception | | `tikz-visual-quality` | `.tex` | TikZ diagram visual standards | | `beamer-quarto-sync` | `.tex`, `.qmd` | Auto-sync Beamer edits to Quarto | | `pdf-processing` | `master_supporting_docs/` | Safe large PDF handling | | `proofreading-protocol` | `.tex`, `.qmd`, `quality_reports/` | Propose-first, then apply with approval | | `no-pause-beamer` | `.tex` | No overlay commands in Beamer | | `replication-protocol` | `*.R` | Replicate original results before extending | | `knowledge-base-template` | `.tex`, `.qmd`, `*.R` | Notation/application registry template | | `orchestrator-research` | `*.R`, `explorations/` | Simple orchestrator for research (no multi-round reviews) | | `exploration-folder-protocol` | `explorations/` | Structured sandbox for experimental work | | `exploration-fast-track` | `explorations/` | Lightweight exploration workflow (60/100 threshold) | ### Templates (`templates/`) | Template | What It Does | |----------|-------------| | `session-log.md` | Structured session logging format | | `quality-report.md` | Merge-time quality report format | | `exploration-readme.md` | Exploration project README template | | `archive-readme.md` | Archive documentation template | | `requirements-spec.md` | MUST/SHOULD/MAY requirements framework with clarity status | | `constitutional-governance.md` | Template for defining non-negotiable principles vs. preferences | | `skill-template.md` | Academic skill creation template with domain-specific examples |
--- ## Prerequisites | Tool | Required For | Install | |------|-------------|---------| | [Claude Code](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/overview) | Everything | `npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code` | | XeLaTeX | LaTeX compilation | [TeX Live](https://tug.org/texlive/) or [MacTeX](https://tug.org/mactex/) | | [Quarto](https://quarto.org) | Web slides | [quarto.org/docs/get-started](https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/) | | R | Figures & analysis | [r-project.org](https://www.r-project.org/) | | pdf2svg | TikZ to SVG | `brew install pdf2svg` (macOS) | | [gh CLI](https://cli.github.com/) | PR workflow | `brew install gh` (macOS) | Not all tools are needed — install only what your project uses. Claude Code is the only hard requirement. --- ## Adapting for Your Field 1. **Fill in the knowledge base** (`.claude/rules/knowledge-base-template.md`) with your notation, applications, and design principles 2. **Customize the domain reviewer** (`.claude/agents/domain-reviewer.md`) with review lenses specific to your field 3. **Update the color palette** in your Quarto theme SCSS file — change the color variables at the top 4. **Add field-specific R pitfalls** to `.claude/rules/r-code-conventions.md` 5. **Fill in the lecture mapping** in `.claude/rules/beamer-quarto-sync.md` 6. **Customize the workflow quick reference** (`.claude/WORKFLOW_QUICK_REF.md`) with your non-negotiables and preferences 7. **Set up the exploration folder** (`explorations/`) for experimental work --- ## Additional Resources - [Claude Code Documentation](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/overview) - [Writing a Good CLAUDE.md](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/memory) — official guidance on project memory --- ## Origin This infrastructure was extracted from **Econ 730: Causal Panel Data** at Emory University, developed by Pedro Sant'Anna using Claude Code over 6+ sessions. The course produced 6 complete PhD lecture decks with 800+ slides, interactive Quarto versions with plotly charts, and full R replication packages — all managed through this multi-agent workflow. --- ## License MIT License. Use freely for teaching, research, or any academic purpose.

# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.

## What this repo is
- Academic workflow system that pairs Beamer lecture decks (`Slides/`) with mirrored Quarto RevealJS exports (`Quarto/`) plus shared figures, preambles, and R scripts. The README (`README.md`) and the published guide (`docs/workflow-guide.html`, source in `guide/workflow-guide.qmd`) explain the contractor-style workflow with specialized agents, adversarial QA, and quality scoring.
- Source manuscripts live under `papers/` and are never overwritten directly; generated artifacts go into `output/` (for new drafts) or `docs/` (Quarto site) while intermediary logs live in `quality_reports/` / `templates/` / `scripts/` per the governance rules.

## Commands
```bash
# Beamer compilation (XeLaTeX must run three passes with bibtex between the first two)
cd Slides && TEXINPUTS=../Preambles:$TEXINPUTS xelatex -interaction=nonstopmode LectureXX.tex
cd Slides && BIBINPUTS=..:$BIBINPUTS bibtex LectureXX
cd Slides && TEXINPUTS=../Preambles:$TEXINPUTS xelatex -interaction=nonstopmode LectureXX.tex
cd Slides && TEXINPUTS=../Preambles:$TEXINPUTS xelatex -interaction=nonstopmode LectureXX.tex

# Deploy Quarto site to GitHub Pages / docs/
./scripts/sync_to_docs.sh LectureXX

# Surface-level verification / single test for a Quarto file
python scripts/quality_score.py Quarto/LectureXX_topic.qmd
```
- Replace `LectureXX` with the real filename (e.g., `Lecture01_Strategies.tex`). The same pattern applies to Quarto files.
- Run `python scripts/quality_score.py` whenever you need a quick pass/fail signal before launching the full `/slide-excellence` review.

## Project architecture overview
- **Slides/ + Quarto/**: Beamer `.tex` files are the canonical source; Quarto `.qmd` is a derived export. Quarto styling hooks live in `Quarto/emory-clean.scss`, figure assets in `Figures/`, and shared TeX macros in `Preambles/`.
- **Scripts/** houses reusable automation (`scripts/sync_to_docs.sh`, `scripts/quality_score.py`, plus `scripts/R/` for replication and plotting). Run these via the command-line wrappers documented above.
- **Docs + output + quality_reports/**: Quarto renderings and static site pages are published to `docs/`; new draft outputs go into `output/` (e.g., PDF/HTML builds). Plans, session logs, merge reports, and specs belong in `quality_reports/` (and follow the templates in `templates/`).
- **Governance surface (`.claude/`)**: Rules (`.claude/rules/`) declare expectations (plan-first, orchestrator loop, proofreading gate, QA templates), skills (`.claude/skills/`) expose commands like `/compile-latex`, `/translate-to-quarto`, `/slide-excellence`, and agents (`.claude/agents/`) do specialized reviews (proofreader, pedagogy-reviewer, quarto critic/fixer, domain reviewers, etc.). Hooks (`.claude/hooks/`) keep context snapshots and enforce quality gates.

## Workflow governance for future Claudes
1. **Plan-first workflow** (`.claude/rules/plan-first-workflow.md`): Enter plan mode for anything beyond single-file fixes. Save plans to `quality_reports/plans/YYYY-MM-DD_short-description.md` before implementation, and log ongoing decisions in `quality_reports/session_logs/`. Approved plans trigger the orchestrator loop.
2. **Orchestrator protocol** (`.claude/rules/orchestrator-protocol.md`): After plan approval, switch to contractor mode (implement → verify → review → fix → score, max five rounds). Re-run the loop if quality gates (80/90/95) are not met.
3. **Session logging** (`.claude/rules/session-logging.md`): Capture the initial context after plan approval, incremental design decisions, and an end-of-session summary. Use `templates/session-log.md`.
4. **Quality & notation templates**: Use `templates/requirements-spec.md` for ambiguous tasks, `templates/quality-report.md` for merge-time reporting, and `templates/constitutional-governance.md` when redefining high-level principles.

## What to read first
- `README.md` for onboarding, tool prerequisites (Claude Code CLI, XeLaTeX, Quarto, R, pdf2svg, gh CLI), and the list of agents/skills.
- `docs/workflow-guide.html` (or `guide/workflow-guide.qmd`) for the multi-agent contractor workflow, adversarial QA explanation, and QA thresholds.
- The `.claude/rules/` files mentioned above to understand when rules load (always-on vs path-scoped) and which ones apply to Beamer, Quarto, R scripts, and `ownership` directories.

Keep these conventions in mind: do not create drafts inside `papers/`, only deliver verified artifacts via `output/` or `docs/`, and always consult the skills (`/proofread`, `/slide-excellence`, `/qa-quarto`, `/validate-bib`, `/translate-to-quarto`) before final review. Quality scoring (`scripts/quality_score.py`) is the single-test gate before running multi-agent reviews. Follow the plan/verify/review loop and document every approved plan in `quality_reports/plans/`.

# CLAUDE.MD -- Academic Project Development with Claude Code

<!-- HOW TO USE: Replace [BRACKETED PLACEHOLDERS] with your project info.
     Customize Beamer environments and CSS classes for your theme.
     Keep this file under ~150 lines — Claude loads it every session.
     See the guide at docs/workflow-guide.html for full documentation. -->

**Project:** [YOUR PROJECT NAME]
**Institution:** [YOUR INSTITUTION]
**Branch:** main

---

## Core Principles

- **Plan first** -- enter plan mode before non-trivial tasks; save plans to `quality_reports/plans/`
- **Verify after** -- compile/render and confirm output at the end of every task
- **Single source of truth** -- Beamer `.tex` is authoritative; Quarto `.qmd` derives from it
- **Quality gates** -- nothing ships below 80/100
- **[LEARN] tags** -- when corrected, save `[LEARN:category] wrong → right` to MEMORY.md

---

## Folder Structure

```
[YOUR-PROJECT]/
├── CLAUDE.MD                    # This file
├── .claude/                     # Rules, skills, agents, hooks
├── Bibliography_base.bib        # Centralized bibliography
├── Figures/                     # Figures and images
├── Preambles/header.tex         # LaTeX headers
├── Slides/                      # Beamer .tex files
├── Quarto/                      # RevealJS .qmd files + theme
├── docs/                        # GitHub Pages (auto-generated)
├── scripts/                     # Utility scripts + R code
├── quality_reports/             # Plans, session logs, merge reports
├── explorations/                # Research sandbox (see rules)
├── templates/                   # Session log, quality report templates
└── master_supporting_docs/      # Papers and existing slides
```

---

## Commands

```bash
# LaTeX (3-pass, XeLaTeX only)
cd Slides && TEXINPUTS=../Preambles:$TEXINPUTS xelatex -interaction=nonstopmode file.tex
BIBINPUTS=..:$BIBINPUTS bibtex file
TEXINPUTS=../Preambles:$TEXINPUTS xelatex -interaction=nonstopmode file.tex
TEXINPUTS=../Preambles:$TEXINPUTS xelatex -interaction=nonstopmode file.tex

# Deploy Quarto to GitHub Pages
./scripts/sync_to_docs.sh LectureN

# Quality score
python scripts/quality_score.py Quarto/file.qmd
```

---

## Quality Thresholds

| Score | Gate | Meaning |
|-------|------|---------|
| 80 | Commit | Good enough to save |
| 90 | PR | Ready for deployment |
| 95 | Excellence | Aspirational |

---

## Skills Quick Reference

| Command | What It Does |
|---------|-------------|
| `/compile-latex [file]` | 3-pass XeLaTeX + bibtex |
| `/deploy [LectureN]` | Render Quarto + sync to docs/ |
| `/extract-tikz [LectureN]` | TikZ → PDF → SVG |
| `/proofread [file]` | Grammar/typo/overflow review |
| `/visual-audit [file]` | Slide layout audit |
| `/pedagogy-review [file]` | Narrative, notation, pacing review |
| `/review-r [file]` | R code quality review |
| `/qa-quarto [LectureN]` | Adversarial Quarto vs Beamer QA |
| `/slide-excellence [file]` | Combined multi-agent review |
| `/translate-to-quarto [file]` | Beamer → Quarto translation |
| `/validate-bib` | Cross-reference citations |
| `/devils-advocate` | Challenge slide design |
| `/create-lecture` | Full lecture creation |
| `/commit [msg]` | Stage, commit, PR, merge |
| `/lit-review [topic]` | Literature search + synthesis |
| `/research-ideation [topic]` | Research questions + strategies |
| `/interview-me [topic]` | Interactive research interview |
| `/review-paper [file]` | Manuscript review |
| `/data-analysis [dataset]` | End-to-end R analysis |

     4→
     5→## What this repo is
     6→- Academic workflow system that pairs Beamer lecture decks (`Slides/`) with mirrored Quarto RevealJS exports (`Quarto/`) plus shared figures, preambles, and R scripts. The README (`README.md`) and the published guide (`docs/workflow-guide.html`, source in `guide/workflow-guide.qmd`) explain the contractor-style workflow with specialized agents, adversarial QA, and quality scoring.
     7→- Source manuscripts live under `papers/` and are never overwritten directly; generated artifacts go into `output/` (for new drafts) or `docs/` (Quarto site) while intermediary logs live in `quality_reports/` / `templates/` / `scripts/` per the governance rules.
     8→
     9→## Commands
    10→```bash
    11→# Beamer compilation (XeLaTeX must run three passes with bibtex between the first two)
    12→cd Slides && TEXINPUTS=../Preambles:$TEXINPUTS xelatex -interaction=nonstopmode LectureXX.tex
    13→cd Slides && BIBINPUTS=..:$BIBINPUTS bibtex LectureXX
    14→cd Slides && TEXINPUTS=../Preambles:$TEXINPUTS xelatex -interaction=nonstopmode LectureXX.tex
    15→cd Slides && TEXINPUTS=../Preambles:$TEXINPUTS xelatex -interaction=nonstopmode LectureXX.tex
    16→
    17→# Deploy Quarto site to GitHub Pages / docs/
    18→./scripts/sync_to_docs.sh LectureXX
    19→
    20→# Surface-level verification / single test for a Quarto file
    21→python scripts/quality_score.py Quarto/LectureXX_topic.qmd
    22→```
    23→- Replace `LectureXX` with the real filename (e.g., `Lecture01_Strategies.tex`). The same pattern applies to Quarto files.
    24→- Run `python scripts/quality_score.py` whenever you need a quick pass/fail signal before launching the full `/slide-excellence` review.
    25→
    26→## Project architecture overview
    27→- **Slides/ + Quarto/**: Beamer `.tex` files are the canonical source; Quarto `.qmd` is a derived export. Quarto styling hooks live in `Quarto/emory-clean.scss`, figure assets in `Figures/`, and shared TeX macros in `Preambles/`.
    28→- **Scripts/** houses reusable automation (`scripts/sync_to_docs.sh`, `scripts/quality_score.py`, plus `scripts/R/` for replication and plotting). Run these via the command-line wrappers documented above.
    29→- **Docs + output + quality_reports/**: Quarto renderings and static site pages are published to `docs/`; new draft outputs go into `output/` (e.g., PDF/HTML builds). Plans, session logs, merge reports, and specs belong in `quality_reports/` (and follow the templates in `templates/`).
    30→- **Governance surface (`.claude/`)**: Rules (`.claude/rules/`) declare expectations (plan-first, orchestrator loop, proofreading gate, QA templates), skills (`.claude/skills/`) expose commands like `/compile-latex`, `/translate-to-quarto`, `/slide-excellence`, and agents (`.claude/agents/`) do specialized reviews (proofreader, pedagogy-reviewer, quarto critic/fixer, domain reviewers, etc.). Hooks (`.claude/hooks/`) keep context snapshots and enforce quality gates.
    31→
    32→## Workflow governance for future Claudes
    33→1. **Plan-first workflow** (`.claude/rules/plan-first-workflow.md`): Enter plan mode for anything beyond single-file fixes. Save plans to `quality_reports/plans/YYYY-MM-DD_short-description.md` before implementation, and log ongoing decisions in `quality_reports/session_logs/`. Approved plans trigger the orchestrator loop.
    34→2. **Orchestrator protocol** (`.claude/rules/orchestrator-protocol.md`): After plan approval, switch to contractor mode (implement → verify → review → fix → score, max five rounds). Re-run the loop if quality gates (80/90/95) are not met.
    35→3. **Session logging** (`.claude/rules/session-logging.md`): Capture the initial context after plan approval, incremental design decisions, and an end-of-session summary. Use `templates/session-log.md`.
    36→4. **Quality & notation templates**: Use `templates/requirements-spec.md` for ambiguous tasks, `templates/quality-report.md` for merge-time reporting, and `templates/constitutional-governance.md` when redefining high-level principles.
    37→
    38→## What to read first
    39→- `README.md` for onboarding, tool prerequisites (Claude Code CLI, XeLaTeX, Quarto, R, pdf2svg, gh CLI), and the list of agents/skills.
    40→- `docs/workflow-guide.html` (or `guide/workflow-guide.qmd`) for the multi-agent contractor workflow, adversarial QA explanation, and QA thresholds.
    41→- The `.claude/rules/` files mentioned above to understand when rules load (always-on vs path-scoped) and which ones apply to Beamer, Quarto, R scripts, and `ownership` directories.
    42→
    43→Keep these conventions in mind: do not create drafts inside `papers/`, only deliver verified artifacts via `output/` or `docs/`, and always consult the skills (`/proofread`, `/slide-excellence`, `/qa-quarto`, `/validate-bib`, `/translate-to-quarto`) before final review. Quality scoring (`scripts/quality_score.py`) is the single-test gate before running multi-agent reviews. Follow the plan/verify/review loop and document every approved plan in `quality_reports/plans/`.

# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.

## What this repo is
- Academic workflow system that pairs Beamer lecture decks (`Slides/`) with mirrored Quarto RevealJS exports (`Quarto/`) plus shared figures, preambles, and R scripts. The README (`README.md`) and the published guide (`docs/workflow-guide.html`, source in `guide/workflow-guide.qmd`) explain the contractor-style workflow with specialized agents, adversarial QA, and quality scoring.
- Source manuscripts live under `papers/` and are never overwritten directly; generated artifacts go into `output/` (for new drafts) or `docs/` (Quarto site) while intermediary logs live in `quality_reports/` / `templates/` / `scripts/` per the governance rules.

## Commands
```bash
# Beamer compilation (XeLaTeX must run three passes with bibtex between the first two)
cd Slides && TEXINPUTS=../Preambles:$TEXINPUTS xelatex -interaction=nonstopmode LectureXX.tex
cd Slides && BIBINPUTS=..:$BIBINPUTS bibtex LectureXX
cd Slides && TEXINPUTS=../Preambles:$TEXINPUTS xelatex -interaction=nonstopmode LectureXX.tex
cd Slides && TEXINPUTS=../Preambles:$TEXINPUTS xelatex -interaction=nonstopmode LectureXX.tex

# Deploy Quarto site to GitHub Pages / docs/
./scripts/sync_to_docs.sh LectureXX

# Surface-level verification / single test for a Quarto file
python scripts/quality_score.py Quarto/LectureXX_topic.qmd
```
- Replace `LectureXX` with the real filename (e.g., `Lecture01_Strategies.tex`). The same pattern applies to Quarto files.
- Run `python scripts/quality_score.py` whenever you need a quick pass/fail signal before launching the full `/slide-excellence` review.

## Project architecture overview
- **Slides/ + Quarto/**: Beamer `.tex` files are the canonical source; Quarto `.qmd` is a derived export. Quarto styling hooks live in `Quarto/emory-clean.scss`, figure assets in `Figures/`, and shared TeX macros in `Preambles/`.
- **Scripts/** houses reusable automation (`scripts/sync_to_docs.sh`, `scripts/quality_score.py`, plus `scripts/R/` for replication and plotting). Run these via the command-line wrappers documented above.
- **Docs + output + quality_reports/**: Quarto renderings and static site pages are published to `docs/`; new draft outputs go into `output/` (e.g., PDF/HTML builds). Plans, session logs, merge reports, and specs belong in `quality_reports/` (and follow the templates in `templates/`).
- **Governance surface (`.claude/`)**: Rules (`.claude/rules/`) declare expectations (plan-first, orchestrator loop, proofreading gate, QA templates), skills (`.claude/skills/`) expose commands like `/compile-latex`, `/translate-to-quarto`, `/slide-excellence`, and agents (`.claude/agents/`) do specialized reviews (proofreader, pedagogy-reviewer, quarto critic/fixer, domain reviewers, etc.). Hooks (`.claude/hooks/`) keep context snapshots and enforce quality gates.

## Workflow governance for future Claudes
1. **Plan-first workflow** (`.claude/rules/plan-first-workflow.md`): Enter plan mode for anything beyond single-file fixes. Save plans to `quality_reports/plans/YYYY-MM-DD_short-description.md` before implementation, and log ongoing decisions in `quality_reports/session_logs/`. Approved plans trigger the orchestrator loop.
2. **Orchestrator protocol** (`.claude/rules/orchestrator-protocol.md`): After plan approval, switch to contractor mode (implement → verify → review → fix → score, max five rounds). Re-run the loop if quality gates (80/90/95) are not met.
3. **Session logging** (`.claude/rules/session-logging.md`): Capture the initial context after plan approval, incremental design decisions, and an end-of-session summary. Use `templates/session-log.md`.
4. **Quality & notation templates**: Use `templates/requirements-spec.md` for ambiguous tasks, `templates/quality-report.md` for merge-time reporting, and `templates/constitutional-governance.md` when redefining high-level principles.

## What to read first
- `README.md` for onboarding, tool prerequisites (Claude Code CLI, XeLaTeX, Quarto, R, pdf2svg, gh CLI), and the list of agents/skills.
- `docs/workflow-guide.html` (or `guide/workflow-guide.qmd`) for the multi-agent contractor workflow, adversarial QA explanation, and QA thresholds.
- The `.claude/rules/` files mentioned above to understand when rules load (always-on vs path-scoped) and which ones apply to Beamer, Quarto, R scripts, and `ownership` directories.

Keep these conventions in mind: do not create drafts inside `papers/`, only deliver verified artifacts via `output/` or `docs/`, and always consult the skills (`/proofread`, `/slide-excellence`, `/qa-quarto`, `/validate-bib`, `/translate-to-quarto`) before final review. Quality scoring (`scripts/quality_score.py`) is the single-test gate before running multi-agent reviews. Follow the plan/verify/review loop and document every approved plan in `quality_reports/plans/`.
